unmatched_b: The inability to see red as a color does not necessarily rule out form vision based on red light absorption.
Psychometric curves plotting judged orientation versus angle demonstrate the conserved visual capacity of rats under red light.
ERG measurements identified two sensitivity peaks in Wistar rats: 362 and 502 nm.
Rats were required to categorize the orientation of a solid disk-like object with a circular boundary and raised parallel bars, alternately colored white and black, forming a square-wave grating.
Orientations in the range of 0°-45° were rewarded as horizontal and orientations in the range of 45°-90° as vertical.
The infrared LEDs (854 nm and 930 nm) emitted ~2-50 times higher intensity at shorter wavelength than did the red LEDs.
Performance under illumination by IR LEDs was at chance.
The emission intensities used in this work are many orders of magnitude lower than those used in two-photon studies.
Four male Long-Evans rats were used in the study.
Upon arrival, the rats were 8 weeks old, weighing approximately 250 g, and typically grew to over 600 g over the course of the study.
Rats were water-deprived on days of training/testing to promote motivation in the behavioral task.
During each session, rats received 17-22 mL of pear juice diluted in water (one unit juice: four units water) as reward.
Protocols conformed to international norms and were approved by the Ethics Committee of SISSA and by the Italian Health Ministry (license numbers 569/2015-PR and 570/2015-PR).

unmatched_a: ERG responses of photopic spectral sensitivity curves of photoreceptors of rats and mice identified two sensitivity peaks in Wistar rats: 362 and 502 nm.

A recent study examined the retinal responses mediated by rods and cones of pigmented (Brown Norway) and albino (Wistar) rats in response to monochromatic far-red light of 656 ± 10 nm in both photopic and scotopic settings.
Whether the photoreceptor activation by red light can lead to functionally meaningful signals has not yet been established.
Behaviors may be documented by video while the animal performs without visual cues at 850 nm illumination.
unmatched_b: Four Long-Evans rats were used in the study.
Object generalization across different views is a hallmark of authentic visual perception and was once believed to belong only to primates.
Four male Long-Evans rats from Charles River Laboratories were used in the study.
Experiments were conducted during the light phase of the light/dark cycle.
Rats were 8 weeks old and weighed approximately 250 g upon arrival.
Rats typically grew to over 600 g over the course of the study.
Rats received 17–22 mL of pear juice diluted in water (one unit juice: four units water) as reward during each session.
The stimulus was a black and white square-wave grating within a circular 9.8 cm diameter circumference.
The spatial frequency of the gratings would be 0.075 cycles per degree of visual angle.
Rats have a large depth of focus, from 7 cm to infinity.
The width of the binocular field directly in front of the rat's nose ranges from approximately 40°–110°, depending on head pitch.
When all illumination sources were turned off, the ambient light magnitude was 0 cd/mm².
Duration of training to reach stable performance was typically 4–6 weeks, with one session per day.
In unbiased rats, the point of subjective equality (PSE) should be at 45°.

matched_pairs.claim_a: There is growing interest in the use of rodents for the study of vision, alone or combined with other modalities.
matched_pairs.claim_b: There is growing interest in the use of rodents for the study of vision, alone or combined with other modalities.

matched_pairs.claim_a: Red illumination is often used in reverse light cycle conditions in animal husbandry settings with the assumption that it will not affect rodent circadian rhythms.
matched_pairs.claim_b: Red illumination is often used in reverse light cycle conditions in animal husbandry settings with the assumption that it will not affect rodent circadian rhythms.

matched_pairs.claim_a: Peak spectral absorption of rhodopsin in rods in rats and mice is at 498 nm.
matched_pairs.claim_b: Peak spectral absorption of rhodopsin in rods in rats and mice is at 498 nm.

matched_pairs.claim_a: Rats under broad-wavelength conditions spontaneously recognize an object even when views differ by angle, size, and position.
matched_pairs.claim_b: Rats under broad-wavelength conditions spontaneously recognize an object even when views differ by angle, size, and position.

matched_pairs.claim_a: Red light might act to entrain circadian rhythms, unconscious to the animal.
matched_pairs.claim_b: Red light might act to entrain circadian rhythms, unconscious to the animal.

matched_pairs.claim_a: Mechanisms must exist for converting miniscule quantities of receptor activation into meaningful signals, perhaps through some cross-retina population code.
matched_pairs.claim_b: Mechanisms must exist for converting miniscule quantities of receptor activation into meaningful signals, perhaps through some cross-retina population code.

matched_pairs.claim_a: Rats and mice are dichromats who possess ultraviolet and green cones, but not red cones.
matched_pairs.claim_b: Rats and mice are dichromats who possess ultraviolet and green cones, but not red cones.

matched_pairs.claim_a: Rodents lack red cones.
matched_pairs.claim_b: Rodents lack red cones.

matched_pairs.claim_a: Dark-adapted rats performed the visual categorization task under 626 nm and 652 nm LEDs with accuracy equivalent to that under white light.
matched_pairs.claim_b: Dark-adapted rats performed the visual categorization task under 626 nm and 652 nm LEDs with accuracy equivalent to that under white light.

matched_pairs.claim_a: Rats are not color-blind.
matched_pairs.claim_b: Rats are not color-blind.

matched_pairs.claim_a: Rats perform poorly in discriminating between nearby wavelengths, compared to humans.
matched_pairs.claim_b: Rats perform poorly in discriminating between nearby wavelengths compared to humans.

matched_pairs.claim_a: Rats performed poorly under infrared illumination, comprising 854 nm and 930 nm light.
matched_pairs.claim_b: Rats performed poorly under infrared illumination comprising 854 nm and 930 nm light.

matched_pairs.claim_a: Visual acuity under the red LEDs (626 nm and 652 nm) cannot be explained by unintended 'leakage' toward shorter wavelengths.
matched_pairs.claim_b: Visual acuity under red LEDs (626 nm and 652 nm) cannot be explained by unintended 'leakage' toward shorter wavelengths.

matched_pairs.claim_a: Place-cell studies of rats navigating under dim red light have been conducted, taken as the absence of visual input.
matched_pairs.claim_b: Place-cell studies of rats navigating under dim red light have been conducted with the assumption of absence of visual input.

matched_pairs.claim_a: During each session, rats received 17-22 mL of pear juice diluted in water (one unit juice: four units water) as reward.
matched_pairs.claim_b: Rats received 17–22 mL of pear juice diluted in water (one unit juice: four units water) as reward during each session.

matched_pairs.claim_a: From the inability to see red as a color, it does not necessarily follow that rodents cannot absorb red light through their rod-dominated retina to support form vision.
matched_pairs.claim_b: The inability to see red as a color does not necessarily mean rodents cannot absorb red light through their rod-dominated retina to support form vision.

matched_pairs.claim_a: Average performance was 84 ± 3% under 626 nm illumination (p=0.158 compared to white light).
matched_pairs.claim_b: Average performance was 84 ± 3% under 626 nm illumination with p=0.158 compared to white light.

matched_pairs.claim_a: Rodent behavioral experiments done under red light are believed to place the animal in dark conditions while allowing the experimenter to observe the preparation directly or by video recording.
matched_pairs.claim_b: Rodent behavioral experiments done under red light are believed to place the animal in dark conditions while allowing the experimenter to observe the preparation.

matched_pairs.claim_a: Rats' color sensitivity is based on two sets of cones with peak sensitivities in the range of ultraviolet (UV cones; peak absorption at 358-359 nm) and green (M cones; peak absorption at 509-510 nm).
matched_pairs.claim_b: Rats' color sensitivity is based on two sets of cones with peak sensitivities in the range of ultraviolet (358–359 nm) and green (509–510 nm).

matched_pairs.claim_a: A study showed significant scotopic and photopic ERG responses to red light even at low intensities.
matched_pairs.claim_b: A study by Niklaus et al., 2020 showed significant scotopic and photopic ERG responses to red light even at low intensities.

matched_pairs.claim_a: Rats performed well under peak 729 nm, though clearly diminished with respect to white light.
matched_pairs.claim_b: Rats performed well under peak 729 nm illumination, though clearly diminished with respect to white light.

matched_pairs.claim_a: Average performance was 72 ± 3% under 729 nm (p=0.00 compared to white light).
matched_pairs.claim_b: Average performance was 72 ± 3% under 729 nm illumination with p=0.00 compared to white light.

matched_pairs.claim_a: Average performance was 86 ± 2% under 652 nm (p=0.419 compared to white light).
matched_pairs.claim_b: Average performance was 86 ± 2% under 652 nm illumination with p=0.419 compared to white light.

matched_pairs.claim_a: All rats (N = 4) performed well under white LED illumination.
matched_pairs.claim_b: All four rats performed well under white LED illumination.

matched_pairs.claim_a: Four male Long-Evans rats were used in the study.
matched_pairs.claim_b: Four Long-Evans rats were used in the study.

matched_pairs.claim_a: Both rat strains showed significant scotopic and photopic ERG responses to red light even at low intensities.
matched_pairs.claim_b: Both Brown Norway and Wistar rat strains showed significant scotopic and photopic ERG responses to red light (656 ± 10 nm) even at low intensities.

matched_pairs.claim_a: Such generalization is a hallmark of authentic visual perception and was once believed to belong only to primates.
matched_pairs.claim_b: Object generalization across different views is a hallmark of authentic visual perception and was once believed to belong only to primates.

matched_pairs.claim_a: Upon arrival, the rats were 8 weeks old, weighing approximately 250 g, and typically grew to over 600 g over the course of the study.
matched_pairs.claim_b: Rats were 8 weeks old and weighed approximately 250 g upon arrival.

matched_pairs.claim_a: Rats and mice are assumed to be functionally blind under red light.
matched_pairs.claim_b: Rats should not be assumed to be blind under red light in investigations exploring rodent physiological and behavioral functions.

matched_pairs.claim_a: Long-Evans rats can discriminate visual form under red light of various wavelength bands.
matched_pairs.claim_b: Long-Evans rats can perform visual form discrimination under red light.

matched_pairs.claim_a: Average performance was 50 ± 1% under 930 nm (p=0.316 compared to chance).
matched_pairs.claim_b: Average performance was 50 ± 1% under 930 nm illumination with p=0.316 compared to chance.

matched_pairs.claim_a: Average performance was 53 ± 2% under 854 nm (p=0.257 compared to chance).
matched_pairs.claim_b: Average performance was 53 ± 2% under 854 nm illumination with p=0.257 compared to chance.

matched_pairs.claim_a: ERG measurements identified two sensitivity peaks in Wistar rats: 362 and 502 nm.
matched_pairs.claim_b: Electroretinogram measurements of Wistar rats identified two sensitivity peaks at 362 and 502 nm with no significant response to long wavelength light above 620 nm.

matched_pairs.claim_a: Investigations aiming to explore rodent physiological and behavioral functions in the absence of visual input should not assume red-light blindness.
matched_pairs.claim_b: Animals in studies using red light may acquire more visual input than previously believed.

matched_pairs.claim_a: Rats had to distinguish between horizontal and vertical orientations of a black and white grating.
matched_pairs.claim_b: The bars of the grating would be expected to be resolvable by Long-Evans rats.

matched_pairs.claim_a: In a developmental study of the visual cortex, mice were dark-reared but were allowed very brief daily exposure to red light.
matched_pairs.claim_b: Rats were dark-adapted in a light-free environment for 20–30 min prior to each session.

matched_pairs.claim_a: Psychometric curves plotting judged orientation versus angle demonstrate the conserved visual capacity of rats under red light.
matched_pairs.claim_b: The normal visual acuity of Long-Evans rats has been estimated as ~1 cpd.

matched_pairs.claim_a: Long-Evans rats demonstrated substantial visual form capacity under illumination by LEDs emitting long-wavelength red and far-red light.
matched_pairs.claim_b: The sequence of sessions for each rat was randomized, with each day's session illuminated by either red, infrared, or white LEDs, with equal likelihood.

matched_pairs.claim_a: Rats were required to categorize the orientation of a solid disk-like object with a circular boundary and raised parallel bars, alternately colored white and black, forming a square-wave grating.
matched_pairs.claim_b: The stimulus was a black and white square-wave grating within a circular 9.8 cm diameter circumference.

matched_pairs.claim_a: The present study extends the range of visual perceptual functions for which rats can serve as models.
matched_pairs.claim_b: Rats have a large depth of focus, from 7 cm to infinity.

matched_pairs.claim_a: A complete understanding of the visual processing of these animals is important not only in the design and control of the behavioral and physiological experiments but also to ensure optimal environmental lighting conditions for their well-being in laboratory settings.
matched_pairs.claim_b: Rats were caged in pairs and maintained on a 12/12 hr light/dark cycle.

unmatched_a: The inability to see red as a color does not necessarily rule out form vision based on red light absorption.
ERG responses of photopic spectral sensitivity curves of photoreceptors of rats and mice were measured throughout the UV-visible spectrum (300-700 nm).
No significant ERG response to long wavelength light (above 620 nm) was detected in Wistar rats.
Orientations in the range of 0°-45° were rewarded as horizontal and orientations in the range of 45°-90° as vertical.
The infrared LEDs (854 nm and 930 nm) emitted ~2-50 times higher intensity at shorter wavelength than did the red LEDs.
Performance under illumination by IR LEDs was at chance.

A recent study examined the retinal responses mediated by rods and cones of pigmented (Brown Norway) and albino (Wistar) rats in response to monochromatic far-red light of 656 ± 10 nm in both photopic and scotopic settings.
Whether the photoreceptor activation by red light can lead to functionally meaningful signals has not yet been established.
The emission intensities used in this work are many orders of magnitude lower than those used in two-photon studies.
Behaviors may be documented by video while the animal performs without visual cues at 850 nm illumination.
Rats were water-deprived on days of training/testing to promote motivation in the behavioral task.
Protocols conformed to international norms and were approved by the Ethics Committee of SISSA and by the Italian Health Ministry (license numbers 569/2015-PR and 570/2015-PR).
unmatched_b: Four male Long-Evans rats from Charles River Laboratories were used in the study.
Experiments were conducted during the light phase of the light/dark cycle.
Rats typically grew to over 600 g over the course of the study.
The spatial frequency of the gratings would be 0.075 cycles per degree of visual angle.
The width of the binocular field directly in front of the rat's nose ranges from approximately 40°–110°, depending on head pitch.
The 117° stimulus should completely cover the rat's binocular visual field.
When all illumination sources were turned off, the ambient light magnitude was 0 cd/mm².
Duration of training to reach stable performance was typically 4–6 weeks, with one session per day.
In unbiased rats, the point of subjective equality (PSE) should be at 45°.
